neighborhoods.name: Greenville Mill Villages
primary_groups: White farm families from SC upcountry — Anderson, Pickens, Oconee, Laurens, Greenville counties
neighborhoods.character: Greenville became the buckle of the Southern textile belt. Mill villages lined the Reedy River and its tributaries — Monaghan, Judson, Brandon, Woodside, Poe, Sans Souci. Each village was a self-contained community owned entirely by the mill company: houses, church, school, company store. Workers walked to the mill from houses that cost $0.25 per room per week in rent. The workforce was exclusively white — Black workers were barred from production jobs. Families who had been tenant farmers or small landowners in the worn-out upcountry soil traded agricultural uncertainty for industrial wages that were among the lowest in American manufacturing.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} moved from {origin_county} to the Greenville mills in the {decade}s, the {surname} family joined a world where the company owned everything — the house, the store, the church pew, and the mill whistle that called them to work before dawn.

neighborhoods.name: Spartanburg Mill District
primary_groups: White farm families from upcountry SC and western NC counties
neighborhoods.character: Spartanburg County had more textile mills per capita than anywhere in the world by 1900. The Spartan Mills, Beaumont, Clifton, Pacolet, and Tucapau villages stretched along the streams that powered the earliest mills. Spartanburg's mill villages were slightly smaller and more scattered than Greenville's, often clustered around a single mill on a creek. The 1903 Pacolet flood destroyed several mills and villages along the Pacolet River, killing over 60 people — a catastrophe that reshaped the local industry.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Spartanburg's mills grew along every creek that could turn a waterwheel — if {ancestor_name} came to work in the Spartanburg district, the {surname} family likely lived within earshot of the mill that employed them, in a village that bore the mill's name.

neighborhoods.name: Anderson-Belton Mill Area
primary_groups: White farm families from Anderson, Abbeville, and Oconee counties
neighborhoods.character: Anderson County's mills developed slightly later than Greenville's and Spartanburg's but followed the same pattern — upcountry farm families drawn to cash wages as cotton agriculture declined. The Anderson Cotton Mill, Orr Cotton Mills, and Belton mills formed a secondary cluster in the western Piedmont. Anderson was also an early adopter of hydroelectric power, earning the nickname 'Electric City' — the same water power that ran the mills also generated electricity.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Anderson was the 'Electric City' — if {ancestor_name} arrived in the {decade}s, the {surname} family came to a place where the same creeks that powered the textile mills also lit the streetlights, a small marvel for families who had lived by kerosene lamp.

neighborhoods.name: Piedmont Manufacturing Company Village
primary_groups: White farm families from Greenville and Anderson counties
neighborhoods.character: The town of Piedmont itself was built around the Piedmont Manufacturing Company, one of the earliest and largest upcountry mills, established in 1876 on the Saluda River. The village was a model company town — planned housing, churches, a school, and recreational facilities all provided by the company. Piedmont Manufacturing employed over 1,000 workers at its peak and was the economic engine of the entire community. The company's paternalism was both genuine and controlling — workers received services but lived entirely at the company's discretion.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Piedmont was where the upcountry mill world began — if {ancestor_name} worked at Piedmont Manufacturing in the {decade}s, the {surname} family lived in one of the South's earliest and most complete company towns, where the mill owner's word was law and the company's benevolence came with strings attached.

major_employers.name: Textile Mills (Cotton Manufacturing)
major_employers.wages: Adult men earned $0.50-1.00/day in the 1890s, rising to $10-15/week by the 1920s — among the lowest industrial wages in America. Women earned less. Children (common before 1920) earned $0.10-0.25/day. Family wage system meant all working-age members were expected to work.
major_employers.working_conditions: 12-hour shifts were standard until the 1930s. Cotton dust caused byssinosis ('brown lung'). Noise was deafening — workers communicated by lip-reading. Humidity kept artificially high for thread quality. Child labor was rampant until state and federal laws gradually restricted it (SC was among the last states to pass child labor laws).
major_employers.narrative_hook: The mills paid cash wages — something the farm never reliably did — but the price was steep. If {ancestor_name} worked in the spinning room, {pronoun_subject} breathed cotton dust for twelve hours a day in air kept deliberately humid, earning wages that were better than sharecropping but among the lowest in American industry.

major_employers.name: Agriculture (Declining Cotton Economy)
major_employers.wages: Sharecroppers retained 50% of crop value after landlord's share. Cash tenants paid $2-5/acre rent. By the 1890s, many upcountry families could no longer sustain themselves on exhausted soil — the mills offered an escape.
major_employers.working_conditions: The upcountry's thin red clay soil was exhausted by decades of cotton monoculture. Families who had been independent landowners became tenants, then moved to the mills. The transition from farm to factory was the central economic story of the SC Piedmont from 1880-1920.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Before the mills, there was the farm — and by the {decade}s, the farm was failing. If {ancestor_name}'s family had worked {origin_county} land for a generation, they watched the soil give out and the cotton price drop until the mill whistle started to sound like opportunity.

major_employers.name: Railroad and Commerce
major_employers.wages: Railroad laborers earned $1.00-1.50/day. Store clerks earned $5-10/week. Small-town commerce provided an alternative to both farm and factory for a minority of workers.
major_employers.working_conditions: The Southern Railway and smaller lines connected mill towns to cotton markets and suppliers. Railroad work was dangerous — coupling cars, maintaining track — but offered better wages than mills for men willing to travel.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Not everyone went to the mills — if {ancestor_name} found railroad or store work in {decade}s Greenville or Spartanburg, the {surname} family had found a different path off the farm, one that avoided the mill village's total dependence on a single employer.

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Mill company housing — frame cottages of 3-5 rooms, built in rows near the mill. Rent deducted from wages ($0.25 per room per week was standard). Houses came with garden plots and outbuildings for chickens and hogs — mill families maintained rural food habits.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Not ethnic in the traditional sense — the mill villages were almost entirely white, native-born, and Protestant. But families from the same rural county tended to cluster in the same mill village, recreating community networks from the countryside. Kinship ties ran through every village.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Company house (immediate) to better company house (promotion-dependent) to possibly private housing outside the village (rare before 1940). Many families lived in the same company house for decades. Homeownership was uncommon until mills began selling village houses in the 1940s-1960s.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: The SC Piedmont mill workers were not immigrants in the traditional sense — they were native-born white Southerners moving from farm to factory within their own region. But they faced intense class stigma from town residents who called them 'lintheads,' 'factory trash,' and 'cotton mill people.' Mill workers were socially segregated from the town middle class — separate churches, separate schools, separate social life. The stigma lasted generations.
specific_incidents: 1934 General Textile Strike — SC was the most violent theater; 7 strikers killed at Honea Path (Chiquola Mill) on September 6, 1934
1929 strikes at Marion NC (nearby) and Gastonia NC influenced SC mill workers
Company evictions of striking families during 1934 strike
nativist_organizations: Mill owners used anti-union rhetoric that cast organizers as 'outside agitators' and 'communists'
KKK was active in some mill communities in the 1920s, though membership was complex — some workers joined, some were targeted

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Mill Village Baptist Churches
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls, baptism records, marriage records. Many mill village churches were supported financially by the mill company, which built the church and sometimes selected the pastor. Records typically held by individual churches or the SC Baptist Historical Collection at Furman University.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The mill village church was the center of community life outside the factory — if the {surname} family attended the village Baptist church, the membership rolls may document their arrival and their place in the tight-knit world of the mill village.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Company Welfare Programs
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Company records including employee rolls, housing assignments, welfare program records. Some held at SC Department of Archives and History, others at university special collections (Clemson, USC, Furman).
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The mill company kept records of everything — if {ancestor_name} worked in a Greenville or Spartanburg mill, the company's employee rolls, housing records, and payroll ledgers may survive as a detailed portrait of daily life.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Fraternal Orders and Burial Societies
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Mill workers seeking insurance and community
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Lodge membership rolls, insurance records, burial records. Woodmen of the World and Odd Fellows lodges were common in mill villages. Records may survive at local lodges or historical societies.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Fraternal orders gave mill workers something the company didn't — insurance and mutual support controlled by the workers themselves. If {ancestor_name} joined the Woodmen or Odd Fellows, the lodge records may document their life in the mill village.

source_references.author: Jacquelyn Dowd Hall, James Leloudis, Robert Korstad, Mary Murphy, Lu Ann Jones, and Christopher Daly
source_references.title: Like a Family: The Making of a Southern Cotton Mill World
source_references.notes: Definitive oral history of Southern textile mill life, based on extensive interviews with mill workers and their families across the Carolinas. Essential for understanding the culture, labor, and community of the Piedmont mill village.

source_references.author: David Carlton
source_references.title: Mill and Town in South Carolina 1880-1920
source_references.notes: Analysis of the social and political dynamics between mill villages and the town middle class in SC, including the class tensions that shaped Piedmont society.

source_references.author: Brent Glass
source_references.title: The Textile Industry in North Carolina: A History
source_references.notes: While focused on NC, covers the broader Piedmont textile industry including SC operations, technology, and labor patterns that were common across the region.

source_references.author: Bryant Simon
source_references.title: A Fabric of Defeat: The Politics of South Carolina Millhands 1910-1948
source_references.notes: Political history of SC textile workers including the 1934 General Strike, labor organizing, and the complex relationship between mill workers and the New Deal.
